content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of size NNN.

We define the function f(l,r)=∑i=lr−1(Ai−Ai+1)f(l, r) = \sum_{i=l}^{r-1}(A_i-A_{i+1})f(l,r)=∑i=lr−1​(Ai​−Ai+1​), where 1≤l≤r≤N1\le l \le r \le N1≤l≤r≤N.

Note that f(i,i)f(i, i)f(i,i) is defined as 000.

A subarray A[l,r]A[l, r]A[l,r] is considered unstable if f(l,r)≠(Ar−Al)f(l,r) \neq (A_r-A_l)f(l,r)=(Ar​−Al​).

Count the number of unstable subarrays in the array.

Note that the subarray A[l,r]A[l, r]A[l,r] consists of Al,A(l+1),…,A(r−1),ArA_l, A_{(l+1)}, \ldots, A_{(r-1)}, A_rAl​,A(l+1)​,…,A(r−1)​,Ar​.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of elements in the array.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots ,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the count of unstable subarrays.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of unstable subarrays of the given array.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For each test case we count subarrays whose first and last elements differ.
Test1: all three pairs have different ends → 3.
Test2: total pairs = 6, equal‑end pairs are (1,3) and (2,4) → 6‑2 = 4.
Test3: all elements distinct, total pairs = 5·4/2 = 10.

solution.approach:
For any subarray [l, r] (l < r) we have
    f(l, r) = Σ_{i=l}^{r-1} (Ai - Ai+1) = Al - Ar
by telescoping. The subarray is unstable when f(l, r) ≠ (Ar - Al). Since (Ar - Al) = -(Al - Ar), the inequality becomes Al - Ar ≠ -(Al - Ar) ⇔ 2·(Al - Ar) ≠ 0 ⇔ Al ≠ Ar.
Thus a subarray is unstable exactly when its first and last elements are different. All length‑1 subarrays are stable.
The number of subarrays with l < r is totalPairs = N·(N‑1)/2. From these we must subtract the pairs where Al = Ar. For each distinct value v occurring cnt[v] times, the number of pairs with equal ends is cnt[v]·(cnt[v]‑1)/2. The answer is:
    answer = totalPairs - Σ_v cnt[v]·(cnt[v]‑1)/2.
We can compute frequencies with a hash map (or by sorting) in O(N) time per test case.
Edge cases: N = 1 → answer = 0; all elements equal → answer = 0; all elements distinct → answer = totalPairs.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (building the frequency map). The overall complexity is O(Σ N) ≤ 5·10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K) where K is the number of distinct values in the array (≤ N).

code.Python3:
import sys
from collections import Counter

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        freq = Counter(arr)
        total = N * (N - 1) // 2
        same = sum(c * (c - 1) // 2 for c in freq.values())
        out_lines.append(str(total - same))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
